sections.heading: INITIAL REPORT IN THE FIELD OF DENTISTRY

elements.text: THIS IS AN EXAMINATION REPORT. THIS REPORT WILL BE INCORPORATED INTO THE PRIMARY TREATING PHYSICIAN’S PERMANENT AND STATIONARY REPORT AND, AS SUCH, WILL BE RELIED UPON AND WILL BE PART OF THE PRIMARY TREATING PHYSICIAN’S MEDICAL-LEGAL OPINION. USE OF THE CONTENTS OF THIS REPORT AND ITS OPINIONS AND CONCLUSIONS ARE FOR PROVING OR DISPROVING A CONTESTED CLAIM.

This examination is an extensive oral cranial examination in that one hour was spent in evaluating Ms. Carraway, ¼ hour was spent reviewing records, one hour was spent in interpretation of the diagnostic findings, and issues of medical causation are discussed.
I base my conclusions on the history given by the patient, clinical examination of the patient, diagnostic tests, and review of any medical records made available at the time of examination.

Demand is hereby made for service of all medical reports and records relating to this claim, pursuant to CCR 10635(c), to be sent to my office for review and comment, as necessary. If no medical records or reports are forwarded for my review, then the parties cannot claim that my reporting is not considered substantial medical evidence by virtue of disregarding this demand.

I hereby reserve the right to change and/or alter any of my opinions, statements, and conclusions stated herein, following the provision of any new medical and/or dental records not provided at the time of this examination.
Ms. Carraway states she worked as an Assistant Nurse Manager for Providence Health.  She worked 12+ hours per day, 4-5 days per week.

Ms. Carraway states her job entailed prolonged sitting, walking, standing, squatting, twisting, bending forward, side bending, reaching below shoulder level, reaching above shoulder level, pushing, pulling, gripping, grasping, lifting up to a maximum of  50 lbs, and carrying up to a maximum of 50 lbs.  She was involved in typing and had computer monitors located to her front and right side.  She used a computer mouse in her right hand and cradled a phone on her left side.
Ms.  Carraway states that she is disabled and has not returned to work since August 27, 2025.
Ms. Carraway states that on 11/20/24, and from CT 3/3/14 to 8/15/25, she was involved in industrial injuries in the course of performing her usual and customary job duties.

Ms. Carraway reports that as a result of performing repetitive work activities such as prolonged sitting, walking, standing, squatting, twisting, bending forward, side bending, reaching below shoulder level, reaching above shoulder level, pushing, pulling, gripping, grasping, lifting up to a maximum of  50 lbs, and carrying up to a maximum of 50 lbs., she developed the gradual onset of pain in her neck, shoulders, right arm, right elbow, right wrist, and in her back.
She states in particular that on 11/20/24, she reported her pain to her employer.

Ms. Carraway rates the severity of pain in her neck as a 9 out of 10, the pain in her shoulders and back as an 8 out of 10, the pain in her right arm as a 7 out of 10, the pain in her right elbow as a 6-7 out of 10, and the pain in her right wrist as a 5 out of 10, all on a VAS.

Ms. Carraway finds that in response to her industrial related orthopedic pain, she developed emotional stressors. Ms. Carraway finds she is clenching her teeth and bracing her facial musculature in response to her orthopedic pain, and also in response to the resultant emotional stressors experienced. This has caused her to develop facial pain.

Ms. Carraway is right hand dominant. Ms. Carraway reports that due to the industrial exposure, she has pain and weakness in her shoulders, arms, elbows, wrists, hands, and fingers. Ms. Carraway reports these orthopedic injuries are compromising and impairing her ability to perform proper and adequate oral hygiene procedures of brushing and flossing of her teeth and gums.
Ms. Carraway states she has gained approximately 15 lbs since her injury.
Ms. Carraway is taking the medication of Ibuprofen, 800 – 1600 mg daily, and Tylenol, 1000-2000 mg daily, on an industrial basis.
She is also using an Albuterol inhaler.

She recalls having her teeth cleaned and x-rays taken at her last dental visit.

sections.heading: CLINICAL EXAMINATION OF THE TEMPOROMANDIBULAR JOINT

elements.text: Physical examination revealed the following:
Crepitus noises palpated and auscultated in the right Temporomandibular Joint upon lateral movements of the mandible
Pain was elicited upon palpation of the:
Lateral condyle of the right temporomandibular joint
Right condylar head via the external auditory meatus

sections.heading: CLINICAL EXAMINATION OF THE MUSCULAR SYSTEM

elements.text: Subjective pain was elicited and objective palpable taut bands were found upon palpation of the:
Left temporalis muscle
Right masseter muscle
Left masseter muscle
Right sternocleidomastoid muscle
Objective classical textbook referral patterns of pain referring pain directly up into the facial areas, occurred upon palpation of the trigger points:
From the right anterior neck Sternocleidomastoid muscle
Ms. Carraway informed us that when we objectively palpated the taut band in her left temporalis muscle and masseter muscles on the right and left sides,  this reproduced her facial pain complaint.
Please Note: According to the State of California Disability Standards, the minimal normal range of motion of opening of the mouth is an interincisal opening of 38.1 mm.
Upon examination, Ms. Carraway was objectively found to exhibit:
Maximum interincisal opening of 50 mm without pain
Maximum right lateral excursion of 7 mm without pain
Maximum left lateral excursion of 7 mm without pain
Maximum protrusion of 3 mm without pain
Deviation upon opening of the mandible to the right side, of 2 mm
Upon examination, I determined Ms. Carraway exhibits:
At maximum intercuspation:	Class I occlusion
Overbite:    3 mm
Overjet:      3 mm
Mandibular tori
Maxillary tori
Missing Teeth #1 and 15
Recession of the gum tissues around tooth #5
Objectively-disclosed bacterial biofilm deposits on her teeth and around her gum tissues

sections.heading: OBJECTIVE CLINICAL FINDINGS CONFIRMING BRUXISM / CLENCHING AND BRACING OF THE FACIAL MUSCULATURE

elements.text: Teeth indentations/Scalloping of the right and left lateral borders of the tongue
Bite mark line/Buccal mucosal ridging of the inner right cheek
Wear on the surfaces of the teeth
The recorded objective data verified and confirmed:
Crepitus sounds were ultrasonically auscultated in the right Temporomandibular Joint upon lateral movements of the mandible
sEMG studies were performed on the masseter, anterior temporalis, sternocleidomastoid, and trapezius muscles and confirmed:
Elevated muscular activity; with incoordination and aberrant function of the facial musculature was confirmed

The muscles of mastication were tested for their functional capacity measuring the maximum bite force and strength of these facial muscles. The measurements were objectively determined with the use of a gnathodynamometer. These measurements objectively determine the maximum bite force of the muscles of mastication used in chewing, comparing one side to the other. The muscles of mastication are innervated by the fifth cranial nerve, the Trigeminal Nerve. The Trigeminal Nerve has a motor function and a sensory function. The motor function of the Trigeminal Nerve controls the bite force of the muscles of mastication. The measurements obtained using a gnathodynamometer objectively demonstrate any unequal biting forces of the muscles bilaterally, and also correlate and confirm the applicant’s complaint of not being able to eat hard and chewy foods by objectively quantifying the amount of the applicant’s bite force.

The objective measurements confirm Ms. Carraway’s complaint of not being able to eat hard and chewy foods because Ms. Carraway could only apply the maximum bite force of 153 Newtons upon biting, when the normal amount of Newton forces to eat hard and chewy foods should exceed 400 Newtons. (400 Newtons = 90 Pounds Force)

The objective measurements also confirm that Ms. Carraway has a weaker force of his bite on one side of her muscles of mastication as compared to the other side. The closing force of her right side measured at 126 Newtons, while closing force of the left side of her closing muscles of mastication was 153 Newtons.
"Gnathodynamometer--a reliable device for assessing jaw bite force."     Shushma, G., Kantly, R. Journal of Evolution of Medical and Dental Sciences, vol. 5, no. 102, 22 Dec. 2016, pp. 7488+
QUANTITATIVE sENSORY tESTING (QST) OF The TRIGEMINAL NERVE

The standard of care in Dentistry (JADA 143(8). September 2012) Quantitative Sensory Testing (QST) was administered to Ms. Carraway. QST measures sensory thresholds for pain, touch, vibration, and temperature sensations and allows access to quantitative, precise clinical findings on sensory nerve function.
The testing results revealed objectively-documented Trigeminal Nerve Neuropathic Pain in the V3 branch of the Trigeminal Nerve which innervates her right TMJ area, and right temporalis muscle area.
Diagnostic Sphenopalatine Ganglion Block

The sphenopalatine ganglion is a branch of the Trigeminal Nerve with the closest proximity to the Trigeminal Nerve as it exits from the brain. Anesthetizing the sphenopalatine ganglion is a direct and accessible way to block the Trigeminal Nerve. A Diagnostic Sphenopalatine Ganglion Block was performed and it was documented that Ms. Carraway’s facial pain is definitely Trigeminal Nerve Pain.
Diagnostic Temperature Gradient Studies
Temperature Gradient Studies performed for Ms. Carraway reveal abnormal temperature readings comparing one side of the facial musculature to the other side.

Diagnostic Autonomic Nervous System Testing, consisting of Pulse Oximetry during the patient’s performance of the Mueller Maneuver, revealed Ms. Carraway’s heart rate instantly and dramatically decreased from 99 beats per minute to 74 beats per minute.
DIAGNOSTIC α-AMYLASE ENZYME ANALYSIS

Diagnostic α-Amylase Analysis, consisting of laboratory spectrophotometric analysis of the amount of α-amylase enzyme present, was performed, and an α-amylase enzyme concentration of 106 KIU / Liter was objectively measured. The spectrophotometric testing is calibrated where normal concentrations of α-amylase enzyme should be less than 45 KIU / Liter.

sections.heading: DIAGNOSTIC SALIVARY FLOW AND BUFFERING TESTS

elements.text: Diagnostic salivary flow and buffering tests revealed:
Observable dry mouth, dry lips, cracked lips
Viscous saliva

The standard of care objective test advocated by the American Dental Association to prove whether definite qualitative changes of the saliva are present or not was performed (and the results photographed).  A tongue depressor adheres to the inside of Ms. Carraway’s cheeks, which conclusively document with certainty that Ms. Carraway does indeed have qualitative changes in her saliva, which has resulted in the increased surface tension of the salivary liquid.

“In addition to conducting visual inspection, the dentist can use a tongue blade to assess tissue dryness. To do so, he or she places a dry tongue blade against the buccal mucosa; if the tongue blade adheres to the mucosa while the dentist attempts to lift it away, this is an indication of tissue dryness and reduced salivary secretion. (Journal of the American Dental Association, Vol. 134, July 2003, Page 615).

Stress, as well as the side effect of medications, causes xerostomia which manifests as qualitative changes of the saliva.  Saliva in its proper form is anti-mutagenic which means it helps decrease the onset of oral cancer. When saliva has been quantitatively changed due to the side effect of medications and/or due to the anti-cholinergic side effect of stress, it has been scientifically documented to contribute to the more rapid onset and proliferation of oral cancer. DFV examines the oral tissues through auto-fluorescence imaging which can detect changes in the oral tissues before any changes are detected by the naked eye.
Ms. Carraway has been examined using DFV, and was found to be negative for any changes in the oral tissues.
To be determined when Ms. Carraway returns to our Hawthorne, Reseda, and/or Anaheim offices for x-rays or presents us with x-rays taken at another dental office.

Diagnostic photographs document scalloping on the lateral borders of the tongue. The photographs document a bite mark line on the inside of the right cheek. The photographs document wear on the surfaces of the teeth. The photographs document the Xerostomia / Anti-Cholinergic side effect where, due to qualitative changes of the saliva, a tongue depressor sticks to the inside of the cheek - even when not held by hand.  The photographs document receding gum tissues as well as objectively-disclosed bacterial biofilm deposits on Ms. Carraway’s teeth and around her gum tissues.

**Of the medical records provided for review, only the material immediately following was sufficiently significant to be quoted/paraphrased. The remainder of the material reviewed was either irrelevant to the decisions required today, or was redundant to the information presented in this section. **
Doctors First Report of Occupational Injury or Illness
From Samir F. Daher, DC, dated 11/04/2025.

Repetitive work duties, multitasking, poor posture, non-ergonomic workstation and stressful working environment caused her to develop pain in her neck, back, right shoulder, right elbow and wrist. Developed nicely sleep disturbances from the persistent pain.
Subjective complaints
Nightly sleep disturbances.
Diagnosis
Cervical spine S/S

Lumbar spine S/S

Right elbow, S/S
Bilateral wrist S/S
Nightly sleep disturbances

Glendale Urgency Chiropractic Clinic
Comprehensive initial evaluation and treatment plan dated, November 4, 2025.
History of injury 11/20/2024
Miss Carraway had been experiencing pain in her neck, back, right shoulder, right elbow and wrist, which worsened on 11/20/2024 and she opted to report her symptoms to management.
History of injury, CT 03.03.2014–08.15.2025
Approximately in June 2022, she experienced a gradual onset of lower back pain, which she related to repetitive work duties. She managed to continue working.

Ms. Caraway suffered a work-related injury in 2012 to her number spine. She was treated with denies retaining legal counsel or receiving any monetary compensation. Ms. Carraway was involved in a car accident over 20 years ago. She suffered neck, injuries, treated and retained beagle council. She fully recovered from her injuries. Ms. Carraway is currently obtaining care for a brain tumor. Ms. Caraway underwent an umbilical herniorrhaphy in 1975, and vertical sleeve gastric Domi in October 2017, a left knee menace in April 2017, a right hemi colectomy in December 2018., And a uterine LEI myomectomy in July 2020. Ms. Carraway does not smoke.
Patient reports
Nightly sleep disturbances.
Diagnosis
Nightly sleep disturbances
Treatment plan
The patient is recommended to undergo the dental evaluation
After a review of Ms. Carraway's history, clinical examination, and a review of records, the diagnoses are:
F45.8		Aggravated Bruxism / Clenching and Grinding of the Teeth and
Bracing of the Facial Muscles of Mastication
M79.1		Myalgia of the Facial Muscles of Mastication
G50.0		Trigeminal Nerve Pain / Central Sensitization
M65.80	Capsulitis; Inflammation of the Right Temporomandibular Joint
M26.69	Osteoarthritis of the Right TMJ
K05.6		Aggravated Inflammation of the Gums

Upon examination, I found Ms. Carraway presents with objective clinical findings of bruxism where Ms. Carraway is clenching and bracing her facial musculature. The objective findings were teeth indentations/scalloping on the lateral borders of the tongue bilaterally as well as a bite mark line on the inside of the right cheek. Ms. Carraway also presents with wear of the surfaces of her teeth.   Upon examination, her facial muscles of mastication, which are involved in bruxism, were found to be painful upon palpation and I objectively palpated taut bands within her facial muscles of mastication.

Ms. Carraway may not have been aware that she had prior episodes of bruxism which can be due to obstructions of the airway during sleep or due to a prior non-industrial habit of bruxism due to extra-pyramidal occurrences in the brain. These preexisting episodes of bruxism can be present and not necessarily cause any facial pain, headaches, or TMJ area pain.

However, it is with reasonable medical probability that the industrial exposure which caused Ms. Carraway to have resultant orthopedic pain with resultant stress would have aggravated any preexisting bruxism by at least 1% The scientific literature has documented that a person can have bruxism in response to pain. Ms. Carraway reports she finds she is clenching her teeth and bracing her facial musculature in response to her orthopedic pain and in response to stress.
“Patients who experience deep pain input seem to find themselves clenching more.”
Management of Temporomandibular Disorders and Occlusion. Okeson, JP. Elsevier Publishing Co. 2013, page 305.
"Many patients will increase their level of tooth clenching and grinding when they experience emotional stress, psychological imbalance or pain."
http://www.aaop.org/content.aspx?page_id=22&club_id=508439&module_id=108085
American Dental Association’s Specialty Board of Orofacial Pain --American Academy of Orofacial Pain

“According to Seraidarian et al., pain state, mainly chronic, has been described by the literature as a factor associated with bruxism. The results of this present study corroborate this finding because we verified a statistically significant correlation between sleep bruxism and chronic pain (p < 0.001). It is noteworthy to mention that 52% of the patients with sleep bruxism exhibited chronic pain.”

Evaluation of correlation among sleep bruxism and depression levels, chronic pain and nonspecific physical symptoms according to axis II of the Research Diagnostic Criteria/Temporomandibular Disorders. RSBO. 2014 Oct-Dec;11(4):352-9 Dias et al.
Ms. Carraway states her resultant facial and jaw pain have caused increased stress.

It is with reasonable medical probability that the industrial exposure which caused Ms. Carraway to have resultant stress would have aggravated any preexisting bruxism. The scientific literature has documented that a person can have bruxism in response to stress.
“It’s called bruxism, and often it happens as we sleep, caused not just by stress and anxiety...”
Position paper of the American Dental Association
http://www.ada.org/public/topics/grinding.asp
“A major cause of Bruxism is stress.”
Position paper of the California Dental Association
http://www.cda.org/popup/Grinding_of_Teeth

“Therefore, chronic psychological stress may contribute to symptoms of TMJ diseases by a variety of mechanisms. These may include an increased occurrence of parafunctional habits such as clenching and bruxism that intensify or sustain joint loading.”
Temporomandibular Disorders, an Evidence-Based Approach to Diagnosis and Treatment, Laskin DM, Greene CS, Hylander WL. Quintessence Publishing Co.  page 117
"Many patients will increase their level of tooth clenching and grinding when they experience emotional stress, psychological imbalance or pain."
http://www.aaop.org/content.aspx?page_id=22&club_id=508439&module_id=108085
American Dental Association’s Specialty Board of Orofacial Pain --American Academy of Orofacial Pain

Additionally, Ms. Carraway has been taking the medication of Ibuprofen and Tylenol on an industrial basis. Phase IV clinical studies of the FDA data have documented that each of these medications has a side effect of bruxism.  Phase IV trials are used to detect adverse drug outcomes and monitor drug effectiveness in the real world. These Phase IV clinical studies have been referenced on 600+ medical publications including The Lancet, Mayo Clinic Proceedings, and Nature.
“Bruxism is found among people who take Profen, especially for people who are female, 20-29 old, have been taking the drug for 1 - 6 months.”
“The phase IV clinical study analyzes which people take Profen and have Bruxism.” It is based on reports of 204,671 people who have side effects when taking Profen from the FDA.
Profen and Bruxism - a phase IV clinical study of FDA data
https://www.ehealthme.com/ds/profen/bruxism/
“Bruxism is found among people who take Acetaminophen, especially for people who are female, 30-39 old, have been taking the drug for < 1 month.”.
“The phase IV clinical study analyzes which people take Acetaminophen and have Bruxism.” It is based on reports of 106,002 people who have side effects when taking Acetaminophen from the FDA.
Acetaminophen and Bruxism - a phase IV clinical study of FDA data
https://www.ehealthme.com/ds/acetaminophen/bruxism/

Therefore, it is with reasonable medical probability that Ms. Carraway’s bruxism was contributed to and aggravated by at least 1% in response to her industrial orthopedic pain, any industrial related stress, and as a side effect of the medication she has taken on an industrial basis.

Upon examination, I found Ms. Carraway has Myalgia of her facial muscles of mastication where palpation of the musculature evoked subjective tenderness and there were objective palpable taut bands within the musculature. There were also objective classical textbook referral patterns of pain from her right anterior neck sternocleidomastoid muscle referring pain directly up into the facial areas. A sEMG, which is approved by the American Dental Association to be used as an aid in the diagnosis of Orofacial Disorders, revealed elevated muscular activity with incoordination and aberrant function of the facial musculature.

The standard of care in Dentistry, as published in the Journal of the American Dental Association (143(8) September 2012 and per the AMA Guide Page 331), is that  Quantitative Sensory Testing (QST) should be performed to objectively determine whether Trigeminal Nerve Neuropathic Pain is present.  After performing the standard of care Quantitative Sensory Testing (QST), it was objectively documented that Ms. Carraway facial pain has evolved into Trigeminal Nerve Neuropathic Pain/Central Sensitization neuropathic pain where the testing results revealed objectively-documented Trigeminal Nerve Neuropathic Pain in the V3 branches of the Trigeminal Nerve which innervate the right TMJ area, and right temporalis muscle area.

The objective QST results also confirm Ms. Carraway’s description of her facial pain complaint where she described her facial pain as sharp and electrical which are descriptive indications of neuropathic pain.

The scientific literature has documented that under conditions of prolonged and/or intense noxious stimulation of repeated Bruxism, myalgia of the facial masticatory muscles transforms into Trigeminal Nerve Neuropathic Pain as a result of central sensitization mechanisms and neuroplasticity,  (Journal of the American Dental Association, Vol. 140 pages 1122-1124, September 2009; JADA, Vol. 140, pages 676-678, June 2009)
The scientific literature has also documented that stress induces hyperalgesia / Trigeminal Nerve Neuropathic Pain.

As will be discussed below, there are many industrial related factors which, with reasonable medical probability, at the very least, have contributed to and aggravated  the inflammation of Ms. Carraway’s gums. The scientific literature has documented that the inflammatory processes of gum disease cause neuroinflammation of the glial cells in the trigeminal nerve and induce Orofacial Pain and Trigeminal Nerve Pain.

The most definitive noninvasive approach in determining objective changes in neural processing and responsiveness within the central nervous system in humans is the use of functional imaging techniques.

I recommend Ms. Carraway be referred for a Functional MRI (fMRI) of her thalamus and brain regions which will help determine the proper long term treatment Ms. Carraway will require for her industrially-caused headaches and facial / TMJ area pain.
I also recommend Ms. Carraway be referred for Neurological examination and treatment as necessary on an industrial related basis.

Upon examination, I found Ms. Carraway has Osteoarthritis within the temporomandibular joint where crepitus sounds were palpated and auscultated within the right temporomandibular joint upon movements of the mandible. Crepitus sounds are indicative of bony surfaces rubbing against each other causing osteoarthrotic remodeling and degeneration of the joints. An Ultrasonic Doppler Analysis, which is approved by the American Dental Association to be used as an aid in the diagnosis of Orofacial Disorders, verified and confirmed the crepitus within the right temporomandibular joint upon lateral movements of the mandible.

Upon examination, I found Ms. Carraway has Capsulitis of her right temporomandibular joint where palpation of the lateral pole of the condyle evoked tenderness which denotes edema and inflammation of the joint.
Upon oral examination, I found Ms. Carraway’s periodontal health reveals recession of the gum tissues, which are objective findings of Ms. Carraway having inflammation of the gums.

Even if Ms. Carraway had prior inflammation of the gums due to smoking, prior gastric reflux, and dental neglect, there are numerous industrial related factors that, with reasonable medical probability, would be contributing to, aggravating, accelerating and/or lighting up any prior inflammation of the gums by at least 1%.

As discussed above, Ms. Carraway states she has industrial related injuries to her shoulders, arms, elbows, wrists, hands, and fingers. Proper oral hygiene of brushing and flossing of the teeth and gums requires fine hand manipulation and manual dexterity using both hands raised up to the mouth. Given Ms. Carraway’s industrial injuries, coupled with her indication that she experiences difficulty brushing and flossing her teeth due to facial pain, it is with reasonable medical probability that Ms. Carraway is not performing proper and adequate oral hygiene. Therefore, with reasonable medical probability, the inflammation of Ms. Carraway’s gums has been contributed to by the industrial injury.

Evaluation of Ms. Carraway’s saliva reveals objective evidence of qualitative changes of the saliva where a tongue depressor sticks to the inside of her cheeks. Ms. Carraway is taking the medication of Ibuprofen which is documented to have the major adverse and key side effect of causing qualitative changes of the saliva. Stress also causes qualitative changes of the saliva. These qualitative changes of the saliva have been documented in the scientific literature to contribute to dental decay and inflammation of the gums. (Position Paper of the American Dental Association’s Council of Scientific Affairs. Journal of the American Dental Association, Vol. 134, July 2003)

The scientific literature has also documented that inflammation of the gums can be aggravated by stress, increased production of salivary amylase caused by stress and pain, a loss of sleep, gastric acids refluxed up into the oral environment, and the body’s increased production of cortisol due to pain. The scientific literature has also documented that bruxism aggravates inflammation of the gums.

An examining doctor’s opinion for causation must be based on a reliable differential diagnosis formed after taking a history, physical examination, diagnostic tests and, if available, a review of prior medical records. I have considered this for Ms. Carraway.
The reader must remember: Treatment is not apportioned!

sections.heading: TEMPORAL CONNECTION BETWEEN INDUSTRIAL EXPOSURE AND THE SUBSEQUENT INJURIES IN MY AREA OF EXPERTISE

elements.text: In my discussion section above, I discussed how, with reasonable medical probability, the industrial exposure and its resultant orthopedic pain and emotional stressors have developed consequential and derivative injuries for Ms. Carraway in the area of Dentistry.

As explained above, even if the medical records reveal Ms. Carraway had these dental problems prior to the industrial exposure, it is with reasonable medical probability that her dental problems were contributed to and aggravated on an industrial basis by at least 1% in response to her industrial pain, any resultant stress, and the side effect of medications taken on an industrial related basis.

While I understand that one of the arguments a person may bring up is latency of when these dental problems started to occur, one has to understand that latency is individual and depends on the intensity of exposure and predilection of the individual.

Based on the methodology used by dental physicians in the field of Dentistry, I have: 1) Shown data to support generic causation; 2) Ruled out other well-accepted risk factors in the patient; and 3) Shown a clear temporal relationship between the industrial exposure and trauma, resultant emotional stressors, side effects of the Ibuprofen and Tylenol medication taken on an industrial basis, and the diagnoses presented regarding Ms. Carraway.

Based on the available data, the history provided to me by Ms. Carraway, my objective findings, the scientific literature, and my expertise in the field, it is my opinion that, with reasonable medical probability, Ms. Carraway’s presenting complaints and clinical symptoms in my area of expertise were caused or aggravated on an industrial basis.

I observed Ms. Carraway during the course of this evaluation. A comfortable doctor-patient relationship was established which enabled Ms. Carraway to candidly confide the difficulties she has experienced as a result of her industrial injuries. I found Ms. Carraway to be a candid and sincere individual. I believe Ms. Carraway has done her best to provide an accurate account of her conditions. Ms. Carraway’s subjective complaints matched the objective findings in my evaluation of her. Ms. Carraway’s history of industrial injury and the symptoms she developed have been consistent throughout the medical records provided for my review. In summation, I find no basis for questioning Ms. Carraway’s credibility. It is evident she suffered dental related injuries as a result of her industrial injuries.

Please Note: I researched the ACOEM guidelines, the MTUS guidelines, and the ODG guidelines, where, to the best of my knowledge, none of these guidelines discuss the treatment of inflammation of the gums, and treatment with an Orofacial Pain Occlusal Orthotic Device.

Section 4604.5(d) of the Labor Code states: “for all injuries not covered by the official utilization schedule adopted pursuant to Section 5307.27, authorized treatment shall be in accordance with other evidence-based medical treatment guidelines that are recognized generally by the national medical community and scientifically based.

The dental specialty of Orofacial Pain has been established by the American Dental Association, and its specialty association has published standard of care guidelines for the assessment, diagnosis, and management of Orofacial Pain in the following text:

Orofacial Pain Guidelines for Assessment, Diagnosis, and Management, Sixth Edition. Leeuw, Reny de, editor. | Klasser, Gary D., editor.  American Academy of Orofacial Pain.2018 Quintessence Publishing Co, Inc.
On page 170 it states:

“Conservative (ie. reversible), noninvasive treatment such as self-management instructions, behavioral modification, physical therapy, medications, and orthopedic appliances are endorsed for the initial care of nearly all TMDs.”
Greene CS. Managing the care of patients with temporomandibular disorders: A new guideline for care. J Am Dent Assoc 2010; 141:1086–1088
On page 181 of the Sixth Edition of the Orofacial Pain Guidelines for Assessment, Diagnosis. and Management published by the American Academy of Orofacial Pain, it states:

“Orthopedic appliances, including interocclusal splints, orthotics, orthoses, bite guards, bite planes, night guards, or bruxism appliances, are routinely used in the treatment of TMDs. Based on current theory, removable acrylic resin appliances that cover the teeth have traditionally been used to alter occlusal relationships and to redistribute occlusal forces, to prevent wear and mobility of the teeth, to reduce bruxism and parafunction, to treat masticatory muscle pain and dysfunction, to treat painful TMJs, and to alter structural relationships in the TMJ.”
Treatment Plan for Ms. Carraway
Orofacial Pain Occlusal Orthotic Device for Daytime Use

The primary purpose of the Orofacial Pain Occlusal Orthotic Device is to relieve facial muscle, ligament, and nerve impingement. This appliance serves to maintain the stomatognathic musculature at their proper resting length, from origin to insertion, thus decreasing pain and improving function. The appliance also decreases the inter-capsular joint pressure placed upon the TMJ from clenching and bracing of the facial musculature. The appliance also protects wearing down and fracture of the teeth from the extreme pressures placed on the teeth from bruxism. This appliance is fabricated and customized in our in-house dental laboratory with a multiple-day process inclusive of in-person adjustments as necessary.
Treatments for Inflammation of the Gums
Dental treatment of Scaling of the teeth and gums with fluoride and Saliva Substitutes due to industrially-aggravated inflammation of the gums.
The standard of care of the American Dental Association requires periodontal maintenance to be performed at least every 3 months.
Craniofacial Exercises
Trigger Point Injections into musculature
Physical Medicine Modalities as needed
Full mouth series radiographs
Panorex

Radiological Referral for Functional MRI (fMRI) of the Thalamus and Brain. An RFA requesting this fMRI will be submitted along with this report. (The benefits of which are discussed in the Chronic Pain Medical Treatment Guidelines, 8 C.C.R. 9792.20-9792.26, Pg. 48 of 127)
Internal / Occupational Medicine Consultation and Monitoring

Bacteria and inflammatory processes from inflammation of the gums readily enter the bloodstream and have been implicated in an increased risk of heart problems, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, stroke, arthritic conditions, kidney disease, and diabetes, as well as a significantly increased risk for the development of cancer throughout the body.
Therefore, Ms. Carraway will require monitoring throughout her lifetime with treatment as necessary on an industrial basis.
Pulmonologist Evaluation

I recommend Ms. Carraway be referred to an Internal Medicine physician specializing in Pulmonology and/or Sleep Medicine in order to assess her sleep condition and to also determine whether she would benefit from undergoing Polysomnographic Studies to prove/disprove AOE/COE, for diagnosis purposes, to determine any impairments or apportionment (if necessary), and to recommend treatment (as necessary) on an industrial related basis.
Psychological Examination and Treatment
To address Ms. Carraway’s complaints of emotional stressors.
Trigeminal Nerve Neuropathic Facial Pain Treatment

Ms. Carraway objectively presents with industrial related facial musculature problems which this office will treat on an industrial basis.  However, as discussed above, Ms. Carraway also presents with Trigeminal Nerve Neuropathic components to her facial pain. This facial neuropathic pain component may arise from the scientific findings that continued muscular pain and continued Bruxism, as well as stress, opioid use, and inflammation of the gums induces Trigeminal Nerve Neuropathic Pain/Central Sensitization. Ms. Carraway requires care that should be provided by a Neurologist with expertise in Trigeminal Nerve Neuropathic Facial Pain.
Duration of Treatment Frequency of Treatment
3 months
1 time per month
Estimated date of completion of treatment:     April 15, 2026
Disability Status in My Area of Expertise
Ms. Carraway is Temporarily Partially Disabled due to her complaint of headaches.
Ms. Carraway has not been discharged.
The condition is not Permanent and Stationary.
Preclusions/Work Restrictions in my Area of Expertise

Since Ms. Carraway suffers from Myalgia of the facial musculature, activities should be prophylactically restricted so as not to aggravate her musculature. The work restrictions are designed to avoid or prevent undue pain, avoid causing an increase in symptoms that would lead to a longer period of temporary disability, avoid causing increased permanent disability, and prevent exacerbation that would increase the need for medical care.
These activities include:
Avoidance of a cold environment which will cause increased myalgia and Trigeminal
Nerve Neuropathic Pain
Avoidance of cradling a phone by bending the head to the side
Avoidance of extensive talking of 15 minutes straight, without a 10 minute rest
Avoidance of improper posture due to a non-ergonomically designed environment

Since Ms. Carraway is clenching and grinding her teeth and bracing her facial musculature, restrictions involving emotional stress must be given consideration. These restrictions contemplate Ms. Carraway avoiding undue emotional stress and strain.

If I can be of any further assistance, or if you have any questions, please contact our office.

As of 6/01/04, Labor Code 5814 mandates a 25% penalty on the amount of payment unreasonably delayed (10% if self-imposed). Accordingly, it would be requested that the defendant please provide immediate payment.

I request that the claims adjuster please provide copies of all medical records, personnel records, investigative reports or any other relevant discovery materials. These data are essential to evaluating complex matters of causation and apportionment. It would also be appreciated if the claims adjuster would provide notification of any scheduled dental Agreed Medical Examinations, defense dental QME examinations or panel dental QME examinations, and/or any reluctance to make reimbursement for a comprehensive permanent and stationary evaluation from this office. I request that the adjuster please advise this office if the applicant is not an employee, was the initial aggressor, did not timely report the injury, filed a fraudulent claim or was otherwise not legally eligible for benefits. I request that the adjuster please also submit any information relevant to any important upcoming court dates, in particular any expedited hearings or Mandatory Settlement Conferences; and please provide notification of any dentist’s depositions.

If there are any valid objections such that there would not be authorization for the requested treatment at this office, I request that the adjuster please report the basis for such denial within seven days.
